name: Audulomi
dates_notes: Pre-Bādarāyaṇa; cited by name in the Brahma-Sūtra itself (e.g., 1.4.21, 4.4.6); no work survives.
dating_methodology: No autograph or biographical record exists. The dating is bracketed by two evidential walls. Terminus ante quem: Auḍulomi must be earlier than the redaction of the *Brahma-Sūtra*, since Bādarāyaṇa cites him by name as one of three competing authorities at 1.4.20–22. The standard placement of the *Brahma-Sūtra* in roughly the second to first century BCE (Nakamura, *A History of Early Vedānta Philosophy*, 1949/1983, vol. I) therefore fixes a hard upper bound near 100 BCE. Terminus post quem: he must be late enough to engage the developed Upaniṣadic *jīva-Brahman* problem, which presupposes the principal Upaniṣads (*Bṛhadāraṇyaka*, *Chāndogya*) in something close to their final form — conventionally placed in the seventh to fifth centuries BCE. The window 400–100 BCE used here follows Nakamura's reconstruction, refined by Hajime Nakamura's identification of Auḍulomi as a member of the doxographic stratum that the *Brahma-Sūtra* presupposes as already canonical. Note (AI-generated audit): no primary biographical text supports any narrower estimate; the academic consensus dates him as a pre-Bādarāyaṇa teacher whose oral teaching circulated in identifiable form within the ritual-and-Vedānta schools, but every sharper claim is conjectural.
core_thesis:
Auḍulomi's claim concerns what the liberated self *is*, not merely what becomes of it. At Brahma-Sūtra 4.4.6, Bādarāyaṇa records his position in four words: *caitanyena ātmanā iti auḍulomiḥ* — 'as a self [whose mode is] consciousness, says Auḍulomi.' The grammar matters. The first instrumental (*caitanyena*) is the predicative-mode instrumental: the liberated self stands forth *qua* consciousness, with consciousness as its operative aspect. The second word (*ātmanā*) is the instrumental of the substance itself, in apposition. The construction therefore says that the liberated self is not consciousness *plus* embodied attributes, nor consciousness *minus* embodied attributes; it is the self *under the description* consciousness, with all qualifying *upādhi* dropped. The doctrinal force is sharper than later Advaita commentary suggests. Auḍulomi is not asserting that the liberated self acquires consciousness; he is asserting that consciousness is the only mode in which the *ātman* genuinely stands at *mokṣa*, the embodied modes (agency, enjoyership, individuated cognition) being conditions of the bound state and not of the self.

The position has dialectical bite within the *adhikaraṇa* it inhabits. Brahma-Sūtra 4.4.5–7 is a three-cornered debate. Jaimini (4.4.5) holds that the liberated self displays the eight powers (omniscience, *satya-saṅkalpa*, etc.) of an unconditioned Brahman. Auḍulomi (4.4.6) replies that what stands at liberation is consciousness alone, the powers being descriptions of Brahman *qua* world-cause and not of the *ātman* in its own right. Bādarāyaṇa (4.4.7) adjudicates: both descriptions are scriptural, the powers when the cosmological aspect is in view, the bare consciousness when the intrinsic essence is in view. Auḍulomi is therefore the named authority for the *svarūpa*-only reading of the liberated state. He commits to a structural thesis: the *jīva*'s individuating attributes are not constitutive of its being, only its bondage. The same logic recurs at 1.4.21, where Auḍulomi appears in the *jīva-Brahman* cluster with Āśmarathya and Kāśakṛtsna; the position there is consistent — what the Upaniṣadic identity-statements assert is settled at the *mokṣa*-state, where the *upādhi*-bound distinctions resolve into bare conscious selfhood.

What the citation does not preserve is Auḍulomi's argument *for* this view. We are left with the conclusion-grammar, not the demonstrative reasoning. Śaṅkara's *Bhāṣya* on 4.4.6 reads Auḍulomi as harmonizable with strict non-difference (the *caitanya* being numerically one with Brahman); Bhāskara reads the same line as an early *bhedābheda* statement (the consciousness being the modal essence of a self that remains a real *aṃśa* of Brahman); Rāmānuja takes the line as compatible with qualified non-dualism (the *jīva* at liberation manifests its essential *jñāna-svarūpa* without losing its *aṃśa*-status). The historical Auḍulomi cannot adjudicate between these readings; the citation underdetermines them. What can be claimed with confidence is narrower: he held the *svarūpa*-as-consciousness thesis, and the tradition treats that thesis as the canonical authority for any later doctrine that distinguishes the *jīva*'s essential conscious nature from its attribution-bound modes.

engaged_works.title: Positions reconstructed from Brahma-Sūtra citations
engaged_works.ascription_notes: No work survives; positions are reconstructed solely from Brahma-Sūtra references (1.4.21; 4.4.6).
engaged_works.summary: Two sūtra-loci preserve Auḍulomi's view; both are short and grammatically pointed. At Brahma-Sūtra 4.4.6 — *caitanyena ātmanā iti auḍulomiḥ* — the construction is doubly instrumental and reads, with the predicative-instrumental supplied: 'as a self whose mode is consciousness, says Auḍulomi.' The claim is that at liberation the *ātman* manifests under the description *caitanya* alone; the embodied attributes that individuate the bound *jīva* are *upādhi*-conditions, not constituents of selfhood. The tight grammar makes the position unmistakable even in the absence of surrounding argument: liberation is not the addition of new properties but the standing-forth of the self in its essential mode. At Brahma-Sūtra 1.4.21, Auḍulomi appears as the second voice in the *jīva-Brahman* triad, between Āśmarathya's *bhedābheda*-leaning manifestation thesis and Kāśakṛtsna's identity-thesis. Śaṅkara reads the trio as ascending toward the strict non-difference reading; modern philology (Nakamura, *A History of Early Vedānta Philosophy*, vol. I, 1949) reads it as a genuine doxographic record of three distinct pre-Bādarāyaṇa positions, with Auḍulomi occupying the *mokṣa*-state non-difference position specifically. Bādarāyaṇa adjudicates at 4.4.7 (*evam apy upanyāsāt pūrvabhāvād avirodham bādarāyaṇaḥ*) by allowing both Jaimini's powers-description and Auḍulomi's bare-consciousness description as scripturally warranted under different aspects. The position firmly attributable to Auḍulomi is therefore the *svarūpa*-as-consciousness thesis; the further claim that this entails strict numerical identity with Brahman is Śaṅkara's reading, not Auḍulomi's preserved words.
